text: SUBSTRATE PRIMITIVE: Viewport-Aware Click. If the intended target's world position is outside the current viewport bounds [0, 63], you must scroll first before clicking. Choose scroll direction and magnitude to bring the target into viewport with minimum action cost, then click using the viewport-relative coordinate. Two scroll models exist: (1) Camera-based — game has a camera attribute with position that changes with player movement; insert scroll actions before clicking. Applies to bp35, dc22, re86, ar25. (2) Grid-offset side-effect — no camera object; offset changes only as side effect of specific action patterns; reorder existing actions so offset-trigger executes before dependent clicks. Applies to lf52. CRITICAL: coordinates sent to env.step() must be valid viewport positions. The remote competition API rejects out-of-bounds coordinates with HTTP 400 even if the local engine accepts them. This bug cost bp35 a 13% score instead of ~100%. PRECONDITION: this primitive applies only when (a) target is in-world but outside viewport AND (b) selecting the target enables a new state transition. If condition (b) fails (e.g., selected peg has no legal jumps), scrolling to reach it wastes budget.

SUBSTRATE PRIMITIVE: Action Budget Awareness. Every action — including failed or rejected ones — may decrement a resource counter. Monitor the budget. Avoid speculative search that depletes it. When budget_remaining < 20% of budget_total, switch from exploration to exploitation: execute only known-good sequences, no speculative actions, no exploration. In some games (sk48), failed moves still consume budget — test whether invalid actions decrement the counter before assuming they are free. Visual cue: orange or progress bar at screen edge indicates action budget. Applies to sc25, tn36, tu93, sk48, and most games with level timers.

SUBSTRATE PRIMITIVE: Animation Timing. After env.step(), if the response contains multiple frames, wait for the animation to complete before reading game state. Do not base the next action on an intermediate frame. Animation frames do NOT cost action budget — waiting is free. Frame count encodes interaction type: 2-frame animations indicate normal state transitions; 3+ frame animations indicate object interactions such as pass-through, embed, crush, or special mechanics. The extra frames encode WHAT HAPPENED, not just that something happened. Use frame count as a classifier. Applies to sk48 (3-frame pass-through), tu93 (activation animations), and most click games.

SUBSTRATE PRIMITIVE: Click Classification by Pixel Change. After clicking (ACTION6), count changed pixels between the before and after frames to classify what the click did. Under 10 pixels changed: selection or toggle — cheap, usually reversible, safe for exploration. 10-100 pixels changed: significant but contained state change — verify intent before proceeding. Over 100 pixels changed: major state transition — costly, may be irreversible, plan before clicking. This classification helps distinguish safe exploratory clicks from committed game-changing actions. Applies to click-games (ft09, tn36, vc33, lp85) and move+click games (sb26, sc25).

SUBSTRATE PRIMITIVE: Undo Semantics. When ACTION7 (UNDO) is in the available action set, it reverts the most recent state change. Use it for safe exploration: take a speculative action, observe the result, undo if it was not productive. When ACTION7 is NOT available, dead-end recovery requires RESET which is costly. Undo itself may or may not cost an action — test once by taking an action, undoing, and checking if budget decreased by 1 or 2. CAVEAT: undo may not be available in competition mode for scored games — confirm per game via ENVIRONMENT.md before relying on it.

SUBSTRATE PRIMITIVE: Structural Alignment is Not Positional Match. If moving a piece to what looks like the target position does NOT trigger a win, the win condition is STRUCTURAL, not positional. Re-examine relationships: what do the colors, borders, shapes, or orientations of OTHER elements signify? The game may be checking relative arrangement, hierarchy, grouping, or connectivity — not absolute coordinates. In vc33, position alone did not equal win; the game checks structural relationships between elements. In sb26, hierarchical placement matters — parent-child relationships, not just which slot an item is in. This is one of the most common failure modes: the solver builds a correct model of WHERE things go but misses what RELATIONSHIPS the game is actually checking.

SUBSTRATE PRIMITIVE: Directional Ambiguity Resolution. When a game uses UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT actions but the game type is unclear from initial observation, try ONE directional action and observe the result to classify before committing to a strategy. Classification outcomes: if a character visibly moves, this is a MOVEMENT game. If values on screen cycle, this is a VALUE-CYCLE game (like tr87 where UP/DOWN increment/decrement values). If all tiles shift simultaneously, this is a BULK-MOVE game (like lf52). If nothing visible changes, there may be a hidden state change — try another direction or click to check. Cost: 1 action to classify. This is a cheap investment versus building the wrong mental model and wasting many actions on incorrect assumptions.

SUBSTRATE PRIMITIVE: Non-Solid Object Interaction. Objects may not be solid to tools or entities. In sk48, the rail passes THROUGH target blocks instead of pushing them. The visual signature of a pass-through is a 3-frame animation (versus 2-frame for normal extension) and fewer changed pixels than expected. Do not assume collision — test it. If an extension or movement produces fewer pixels changed than expected, the tool may have passed through an object rather than being blocked by it. This is related to the Animation Timing primitive: frame count encodes interaction type. Applies to sk48 and any game with tool-object interactions.

SUBSTRATE PRIMITIVE: Retraction Can Pull. In games with an extend/retract mechanic (rail, rope, arm, etc.), retracting the tool may pull embedded objects toward the head. This is a second use of the same action — the tool doubles as both a pusher (on extend) and a puller (on retract). Test by extending through an object, then retracting, and observing whether the object moves with the tool. The retract-as-pull mechanic enables solutions that are impossible with extend-only thinking. Applies to sk48 and any game with extend/retract mechanics.

SUBSTRATE COMPOSITION RULES. All substrate primitives are loaded into the agent's context at startup. They are constraints on action, not actions themselves. Ordering: substrate primitives are presented BEFORE game-world context in the prompt. They are like physics — learned before specific games, applied to all of them. Override rule: the game-world model can override a substrate primitive for a specific game. The prompt includes: 'The following substrate rules apply UNLESS the game-world model explicitly states otherwise.' No conflict resolution engine is needed. Primitives compose as independent preconditions. Joint constraint satisfaction over a small set of natural-language rules is what language models do well. If two primitives could conflict, they should be merged into a single more-nuanced primitive rather than adding an arbitration layer.
